- What Happens To Your Body on High Protein Diet: Kind: captions Language: en Are you ready to instantly lose&nbsp; weight, gain muscle, and live longer?&nbsp;&nbsp; Well, no matter what anyone says, there is no&nbsp; miracle diet that can do these things for you.&nbsp;&nbsp; Staying healthy is a balance between eating&nbsp; the right foods and being active. However,&nbsp;&nbsp; a high protein diet may be a good place to&nbsp; start. There are a lot of positive things&nbsp;&nbsp; that eating protein can do for your body. But&nbsp; too much protein can cause some nasty side&nbsp;&nbsp; effects. How much is too much? And does it matter&nbsp; where your protein comes from? Let’s find out. Proteins are arguably the most&nbsp; important molecules in your body.&nbsp;&nbsp; They make up your cells, allow your body to carry&nbsp; out essential functions, and without protein,&nbsp;&nbsp; you’d die. There are over 80,000&nbsp; different proteins in the human body,&nbsp;&nbsp; and each one has a function. In fact, proteins&nbsp; make up around 20% of your entire body. So,&nbsp;&nbsp; it should come as no surprise that consuming&nbsp; enough protein is vital to your survival. Proteins are made up of smaller molecules called&nbsp; amino acids that are joined together and aligned&nbsp;&nbsp; in unique ways to create different shapes.&nbsp; The crazy thing is out of the 21 amino acids&nbsp;&nbsp; used by the human body to make proteins; there&nbsp; are 9 that can’t be synthesized by your cells.&nbsp;&nbsp; This means the only way to get them is by eating&nbsp; foods that have these amino acids stored in them. Which foods contain the amino acids and proteins&nbsp; I need to stay alive, you may be wondering.&nbsp;&nbsp; Don’t worry; The Infographics Show&nbsp; has got you covered. We will tell you&nbsp;&nbsp; exactly where to find the protein your body&nbsp; so desperately craves later on in the video. Let’s start with the basics. When you eat&nbsp; something, your body tears it apart, breaks&nbsp;&nbsp; it down, and then circulates the molecules and&nbsp; nutrients contained within the food around your&nbsp;&nbsp; body so that your cells can get what they need.&nbsp; For example, if you eat a slice of pork belly,&nbsp;&nbsp; the fats, proteins, and other molecules&nbsp; that are contained within the piece of meat&nbsp;&nbsp; don’t go straight to your belly. Instead, the meat&nbsp; is broken down in the stomach and intestines, and&nbsp;&nbsp; the individual nutrients get circulated to every&nbsp; part of your body via your circulatory system. Therefore, we eat to provide our bodies&nbsp; with the molecules necessary to function&nbsp;&nbsp; and keep us alive. And proteins are one&nbsp; of the most important molecules of all.&nbsp;&nbsp; So, how much protein should you actually be&nbsp; eating? And what does it do to your body? Everyone’s body is different, which means that&nbsp; everyone's daily intake of protein will need to&nbsp;&nbsp; be different to provide the optimum effect.&nbsp; Researchers suggest that the average male&nbsp;&nbsp; should consume around 56 grams of protein per&nbsp; day while females should have approximately 46&nbsp;&nbsp; grams of protein each day. However, since&nbsp; everyone is a different size and weight,&nbsp;&nbsp; there may be a better way to calculate&nbsp; how much protein you should be eating. You should be consuming around .365 grams of&nbsp; protein a day for every pound you weigh. An easier&nbsp;&nbsp; way to think about this is for every 10 pounds of&nbsp; body weight; you should eat 3.65 grams of protein.&nbsp;&nbsp; This means if you weigh 150 pounds, you should&nbsp; eat around 55 grams of protein throughout your&nbsp;&nbsp; day. However, this is just a baseline. People who&nbsp; are more active will need to consume more protein&nbsp;&nbsp; to maintain or build their muscle mass. But these&nbsp; calculations are a good baseline to start with. Surprisingly, there is still a decent amount of&nbsp; debate within the scientific and medical community&nbsp;&nbsp; on how much protein someone should&nbsp; actually consume in a 24 hour period.&nbsp;&nbsp; Some experts put the number twice&nbsp; as high as previously mentioned,&nbsp;&nbsp; but more research still needs to&nbsp; be done to justify these numbers. Regardless of what the exact amount of&nbsp; protein a person should consume is, there&nbsp;&nbsp; is an even more important question. What does&nbsp; the protein you eat physically do to your body? Since proteins play a vital role in many life&nbsp; functions and literally make up the cells&nbsp;&nbsp; in your body, a better question might be: what&nbsp; don’t proteins do? There are definitely a number&nbsp;&nbsp; of noticeable effects that a high-protein diet&nbsp; can have on your overall health and physiology. Scientists have found that high-protein&nbsp; diets end up reducing your appetite.&nbsp;&nbsp; A number of studies revealed that out of all the&nbsp; nutrients you consume on a daily basis, protein is&nbsp;&nbsp; the one that makes you feel full the fastest.&nbsp; The reason for this is that protein reduces&nbsp;&nbsp; the amount of a hormone called ghrelin in your&nbsp; body. Ghrelin is one of the molecules that tells&nbsp;&nbsp; your brain that you’re hungry. Therefore, the less&nbsp; ghrelin in your system, the less hungry you feel. Protein also boosts the levels of peptide&nbsp; YY, which signals the body that it’s full.&nbsp;&nbsp; With the reduction of ghrelin and the increase of&nbsp; peptide YY, the body feels nutritionally satisfied&nbsp;&nbsp; much sooner than you would with a low-protein&nbsp; meal. This reduces the urge to overeat,&nbsp;&nbsp; which in turn can help you&nbsp; lose weight and stay healthier. In a study conducted at the University&nbsp; of Washington School of Medicine,&nbsp;&nbsp; people who increased the number of calories&nbsp; they consumed from a protein source from 15%&nbsp;&nbsp; to 30% ate around 441 fewer calories per day than&nbsp; someone on a lower-protein diet. The craziest part&nbsp;&nbsp; was that the people in the study were&nbsp; allowed to eat as much as they wanted.&nbsp;&nbsp; The group that increased their protein intake&nbsp; just felt fuller quicker, so they ate less.&nbsp;&nbsp; What this implies is that subbing out foods that&nbsp; are higher in fats, sugars, and carbohydrates&nbsp;&nbsp; for meat or high protein veggies might make&nbsp; you feel more satisfied with fewer calories. Along with suppressing appetite, high protein&nbsp; diets can help you lose weight in another way. High protein intake has been&nbsp; shown to increase metabolism,&nbsp;&nbsp; at least for a short period of time. Even as you&nbsp; eat, your body is using energy to break down the&nbsp;&nbsp; food and circulate the nutrients to your vital&nbsp; organs and muscles. The energy expended and the&nbsp;&nbsp; increase in metabolic rate to aid in digestion&nbsp; is called the thermic effect of food or TEF. The interesting thing is that protein has a much&nbsp; higher thermic effect than most other types of&nbsp;&nbsp; molecules like carbohydrates or fats. Studies&nbsp; have shown that by eating high protein foods,&nbsp;&nbsp; your thermic effect can increase by 5 to&nbsp; 15 percent more than consuming food with&nbsp;&nbsp; little or no protein. What this means&nbsp; is that your body has to work harder&nbsp;&nbsp; to break down protein than other molecules. This&nbsp; causes you to use more energy during digestion&nbsp;&nbsp; while simultaneously increasing your metabolism. All of these factors combined can cause your&nbsp; body to burn 80 to 100 more calories a day&nbsp;&nbsp; just from eating. And this might be a&nbsp; conservative number. One study showed&nbsp;&nbsp; that a group of people eating a high protein&nbsp; diet burned on average 260 calories more per day&nbsp;&nbsp; than a group of people on a low protein diet.&nbsp; This is the equivalent of the amount of energy&nbsp;&nbsp; expended from a moderate workout session. Who&nbsp; knew that eating could be such a good exercise? And speaking of working out, protein is&nbsp; literally the building block for all your&nbsp;&nbsp; muscles. So, does eating more protein&nbsp; correlate to increased muscle size? Not exactly. However, increasing the amount of&nbsp; protein in your diet will impact your muscles&nbsp;&nbsp; in a number of different ways. Eating more&nbsp; protein does not necessarily build more muscle,&nbsp;&nbsp; but it does help maintain muscle mass and&nbsp; promote growth. If all you did was eat protein,&nbsp;&nbsp; your muscles would not magically become&nbsp; bigger. However, if you engaged in a&nbsp;&nbsp; weightlifting regiment on a regular basis&nbsp; while also increasing protein intake,&nbsp;&nbsp; your muscles would likely get larger more&nbsp; quickly than if you stuck to your typical diet. Someone who is active and trying&nbsp; to bulk up their muscle mass should&nbsp;&nbsp; definitely increase the amount of&nbsp; protein they put into their bodies.&nbsp;&nbsp; There is also another important factor to consider&nbsp; when trying to build, or more importantly,&nbsp;&nbsp; maintain muscle mass. If for some reason you&nbsp; need to stop lifting weights or reduce your&nbsp;&nbsp; time working out due to an injury or life just&nbsp; getting in the way, you should still maintain a&nbsp;&nbsp; high protein diet. The rationale for this is that&nbsp; if you go from being highly active to less active,&nbsp;&nbsp; you will begin to lose weight as your muscles&nbsp; shrink. However, a high protein diet can slow&nbsp;&nbsp; the rate of and even prevent muscle loss&nbsp; if the right types of food are consumed. Perhaps the biggest selling point for&nbsp; high-protein diets is that not only&nbsp;&nbsp; will they help you lose weight, but they&nbsp; will help you keep the pounds off as well. Over the course of a year, high-protein diets&nbsp; seem to be much more effective at helping people&nbsp;&nbsp; maintain weight loss. Now, it is important&nbsp; to remember that just eating a lot of protein&nbsp;&nbsp; will not cause you to lose weight instantly.&nbsp; However, there may be some huge benefits if you&nbsp;&nbsp; stay active, eat the right types of protein,&nbsp; and keep track of your calories. For example,&nbsp;&nbsp; a study that tracked 130 people on high-protein&nbsp; diets over the course of a year found that they&nbsp;&nbsp; lost 53% more body fat compared to a control&nbsp; group of people on a regular-protein diet. In a clinical trial conducted by the Department&nbsp; of Human Biology at Maastricht University in the&nbsp;&nbsp; Netherlands, researchers found that high-protein&nbsp; diets reduced weight regain by around 50%.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is huge for people who want to lose weight&nbsp; and keep the weight off. As many dieters know,&nbsp;&nbsp; losing the weight is only half the battle;&nbsp; it’s keeping the weight off that can be hard.&nbsp;&nbsp; But it seems from a number of studies that&nbsp; a high-protein diet and healthy lifestyle&nbsp;&nbsp; may be one of the best ways to do just that. You probably have heard that one side&nbsp; effect of a high protein diet is that&nbsp;&nbsp; it can be damaging to your bones.&nbsp; But is there any truth to this? This myth probably originated from the idea&nbsp; that protein can increase the acidity level of&nbsp;&nbsp; the body slightly, which in theory could cause&nbsp; calcium to leach from your bones. To be fair,&nbsp;&nbsp; if your blood’s acidity was high enough,&nbsp; it most certainly could cause the calcium&nbsp;&nbsp; on your bones to break down and weaken&nbsp; your skeletal system. But if your blood&nbsp;&nbsp; ever ended up at that high level of acidity,&nbsp; you’d be dead, so it really wouldn’t matter. What long-term studies have shown is that&nbsp; both animal and plant protein can actually&nbsp;&nbsp; help people maintain bone mass as they&nbsp; get older. According to some research,&nbsp;&nbsp; people who maintain a high protein diet over&nbsp; extended periods of time also seem to be at a&nbsp;&nbsp; lower risk of osteoporosis and bone fractures.&nbsp; It is important to point out that this does&nbsp;&nbsp; not mean you should immediately start consuming&nbsp; massive quantities of beef to increase your bone&nbsp;&nbsp; health. There are better sources of protein,&nbsp; which we will break down for you in a bit. The same thing goes for this&nbsp; next health benefit. It is all&nbsp;&nbsp; contingent on what type of protein you are eating. It may seem counterintuitive, but a high-protein&nbsp; diet can actually lower your blood pressure.&nbsp;&nbsp; The exact mechanism for this is&nbsp; not completely understood yet,&nbsp;&nbsp; but in one study, it was found that a&nbsp; high-protein diet did not just lower blood&nbsp;&nbsp; pressure but also reduced LDL cholesterol and&nbsp; triglycerides. This is important because these&nbsp;&nbsp; factors in high levels can be a recipe for&nbsp; heart attacks, strokes, and kidney disease. Although a high-protein diet can&nbsp; have harmful effects on the kidneys,&nbsp;&nbsp; you probably don’t need to worry too&nbsp; much as long as you are in good health. Doctors recommend that people with kidney&nbsp; diseases reduce the amount of protein they eat.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is because high amounts of protein&nbsp; can exacerbate problems in kidneys that&nbsp;&nbsp; are already damaged. However, the same&nbsp; thing cannot be said about people with&nbsp;&nbsp; healthy kidneys. It is always&nbsp; important to follow the advice&nbsp;&nbsp; of doctors and medical professionals&nbsp; when they recommend lifestyle changes.&nbsp;&nbsp; However, no studies have connected high protein&nbsp; consumption to causing damage to healthy kidneys. Protein may even be able to reverse&nbsp; and repair the damage done to the body. Since it is the building block of&nbsp; your cells, mussels, and organs,&nbsp;&nbsp; eating more protein actually provides your body&nbsp; with the resources it needs to help repair itself.&nbsp;&nbsp; Several studies have even claimed that after you&nbsp; suffer an injury, consuming more protein could&nbsp;&nbsp; help speed up the time it takes to recover. Some&nbsp; researchers even claim that if you are on a high&nbsp;&nbsp; protein diet before you get injured, it may&nbsp; cause your body to heal faster than someone&nbsp;&nbsp; who is not. More analysis needs to be done, but&nbsp; it is possible that in the future, doctors will&nbsp;&nbsp; recommend foods high in protein after an injury,&nbsp; along with other already established treatments. So, we know that high protein diets have several&nbsp; immediate and short-term benefits for the body.&nbsp;&nbsp; But what about as you get older? It may&nbsp; sound crazy, but a high protein diet in&nbsp;&nbsp; the middle stages of life can have lasting&nbsp; benefits even into your more mature years. It is no secret that as you age, your body&nbsp; becomes weaker. Your muscles start to shrink,&nbsp;&nbsp; and your bones become more frail. However,&nbsp; studies have shown by eating more protein,&nbsp;&nbsp; these effects can be mitigated. It is important&nbsp; that you also maintain some form of activity as&nbsp;&nbsp; well because if you don’t use your muscles,&nbsp; they will begin to atrophy no matter how&nbsp;&nbsp; much protein you eat. But eating a high-protein&nbsp; diet during your life and into your later years&nbsp;&nbsp; may help your body retain some of its strength&nbsp; and keep you in better overall health. There are definitely a lot of positives to eating&nbsp; a high protein diet, but is there a downside?&nbsp;&nbsp; Unfortunately, yes, so before&nbsp; you go on a meat buying spree,&nbsp;&nbsp; make sure to watch the rest of this video. As we said before, there is no miracle diet that&nbsp; can solve all of your weight and health problems.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is especially true of high-protein diets that&nbsp; require you to cut out all other macronutrients&nbsp;&nbsp; from your meals. You should steer clear of any&nbsp; diet that tells you to remove all carbs or fats&nbsp;&nbsp; from your life. In these types of situations,&nbsp; you could develop nutritional deficiencies&nbsp;&nbsp; such as low fiber, which can cause&nbsp; headaches and constipation. Therefore,&nbsp;&nbsp; a high-protein diet does not mean you should&nbsp; remove all other types of food from your life. As we said before, the type of protein you’re&nbsp; eating matters. And there are definitely some&nbsp;&nbsp; foods that are better than others.&nbsp; You should not be eating all red meat&nbsp;&nbsp; or full-fat dairy foods because they can&nbsp; cause an increased risk of heart disease.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is because these types of food are often&nbsp; full of saturated fats and high in cholesterol. So, this brings up the question: what&nbsp; type of foods should you be eating in&nbsp;&nbsp; your high protein diet? You obviously want&nbsp; to receive the benefits of consuming more&nbsp;&nbsp; protein but don't want to put yourself at&nbsp; risk for high cholesterol or heart disease.&nbsp;&nbsp; Luckily, The Infographics&nbsp; Show has got you covered. The best meats to eat to get your&nbsp; protein fix are a mixture of birds,&nbsp;&nbsp; fish, and one other animal that you won’t believe. Chicken is a relatively low-priced meat that is&nbsp; healthy and can provide enough protein to meet&nbsp;&nbsp; your daily requirements. Chicken contains around&nbsp; 31 grams of protein in every 100 grams of meat.&nbsp;&nbsp; The best thing about chicken is&nbsp; that about 90% of the calories&nbsp;&nbsp; in the meat come directly from protein.&nbsp; We discussed earlier how important this&nbsp;&nbsp; is for speeding up your metabolism&nbsp; and reducing daily caloric intake. However, an even healthier option for high protein&nbsp; meat is fish. Yellowfin tuna contains around 30&nbsp;&nbsp; grams of protein per 100 grams of meat. Other&nbsp; fish possess slightly less protein per serving,&nbsp;&nbsp; but not by much. However, the benefit of&nbsp; eating fish as part of a high protein diet&nbsp;&nbsp; is that they also contain omega-3 fatty acids and&nbsp; vitamins such as D and B2. Omega-3 has shown to be&nbsp;&nbsp; extremely good for your heart and reduce plaque&nbsp; buildup around blood vessels. This means that&nbsp;&nbsp; fish is a two-for-one meal that provides both high&nbsp; amounts of protein and essential vitamins as well. Another seafood that is high in protein&nbsp; is shrimp. These crustaceans contain&nbsp;&nbsp; 23 grams of protein for every 100 grams of meat.&nbsp; They also might provide a cheaper way to get&nbsp;&nbsp; your daily protein from seafood as shrimp tend&nbsp; to cost less per pound than higher-end fishes. The most surprising animal that can&nbsp; serve as a good source of protein&nbsp;&nbsp; isn’t a fish, bird, or mammal.&nbsp; Instead, it is an insect. Crickets are an excellent source of protein. They&nbsp; contain around 60 grams of protein for every 100&nbsp;&nbsp; grams of crickets consumed. Right now, crickets&nbsp; tend not to be the main source of protein for any&nbsp;&nbsp; diet. However, as scientists and nutritionists&nbsp; work to make crickets more mainstream,&nbsp;&nbsp; this is changing. The great thing about&nbsp; these insects is that they are plentiful,&nbsp;&nbsp; reproduce rapidly, and have minimal impact on&nbsp; the environment. When they are eaten whole,&nbsp;&nbsp; crickets don’t really have much of a taste other&nbsp; than whatever seasoning is put on them. Some&nbsp;&nbsp; companies are even using crickets in their protein&nbsp; bars and powder. Regardless of your feelings on&nbsp;&nbsp; bugs, crickets may be the most sustainable,&nbsp; cost-effective source of protein we have. Meat is not the only way to get your protein,&nbsp; though. If you are a vegetarian or just looking&nbsp;&nbsp; for plant-based options, there are plenty of&nbsp; high-protein plants that will allow you to&nbsp;&nbsp; meet your daily intake goals. These foods&nbsp; also are healthier than eating most meats&nbsp;&nbsp; and can either be used as a substitute or&nbsp; an extra source of protein during a meal. One of the richest plant proteins is seitan.&nbsp; This durable food is made from the gluten of&nbsp;&nbsp; wheat and contains around 25 grams of&nbsp; protein for every 100 grams of seitan.&nbsp;&nbsp; It also boasts the highest amount of protein per&nbsp; gram out of any plant-based alternatives to meat. Tofu is also high in protein as well.&nbsp; Tofu is actually made of coagulated soy&nbsp;&nbsp; milk that has been pressed together into a block.&nbsp;&nbsp; Although this doesn’t sound super appetizing,&nbsp; Tofu has the ability to fit into almost any dish&nbsp;&nbsp; as it has very little taste of its own and its&nbsp; texture can change based on how it’s cooked.&nbsp;&nbsp; The best part about tofu is that it not only&nbsp; contains high amounts of protein but it is&nbsp;&nbsp; rich in iron and calcium as well. In 100 grams&nbsp; of tofu, there are around 20 grams of protein. Beans are also a great protein source for&nbsp; anyone trying to stay away from meat. Most beans&nbsp;&nbsp; constrain around 15 grams of protein per cup. And&nbsp; since beans can be cooked in a variety of ways,&nbsp;&nbsp; they can easily be added to pretty much&nbsp; any meal to boost your protein intake. Medical professionals recommend that most of&nbsp; your protein should come from plant sources.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is because they tend to be a healthier option&nbsp; than meat due to the fact that plants contain many&nbsp;&nbsp; nutrients and vitamins your body needs. If&nbsp; you only eat meat, you will definitely have&nbsp;&nbsp; a high-protein diet, but that will be about it.&nbsp; There is no substitute for a well-balanced diet,&nbsp;&nbsp; so make sure to incorporate different protein&nbsp; sources in your meals throughout the day. This brings us to a very important&nbsp; question. How much protein is too much?&nbsp;&nbsp; And what side effects can&nbsp; consuming too much protein have? It is always possible to have too much of a&nbsp; good thing in life, and protein is no different.&nbsp;&nbsp; When you consume too much protein the&nbsp; risks outweigh the benefits. For example,&nbsp;&nbsp; although protein has been shown to have&nbsp; no long-term effects on healthy kidneys,&nbsp;&nbsp; it can still cause other problems&nbsp; in this part of the body. Eating&nbsp;&nbsp; too much protein can lead to kidney stones,&nbsp; which are incredibly painful until removed. Depending on the size of the stones, a doctor&nbsp; may need to use a laser to break them up into&nbsp;&nbsp; smaller pieces. There are two main ways&nbsp; that the kidney stones can then be removed;&nbsp;&nbsp; both are painful enough that the patient&nbsp; needs to be given anesthesia. One method is&nbsp;&nbsp; known as a cystoscopy, where the doctor inserts a&nbsp; cystoscope into the urethra to locate and remove&nbsp;&nbsp; the stones. You can imagine that having anything&nbsp; stuck up your urethra would be unpleasant. Another method of removing kidney stones&nbsp; is called percutaneous nephrolithotomy.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is when the doctor cuts a hole in your side&nbsp; and kidney and inserts a tool called a nephroscope&nbsp;&nbsp; into it . This device then allows the doctor&nbsp; to look around and remove any stones they find. As mentioned before, eating too much red meat can&nbsp; also lead to high cholesterol and heart problems.&nbsp;&nbsp; Therefore, it is important not to restrict&nbsp; your high protein diet to just one type&nbsp;&nbsp; of meat or protein source. There is still&nbsp; disagreement amongst medical professionals&nbsp;&nbsp; as to how much protein is too much, but there&nbsp; are definitely some guidelines to follow. For the average person, it is recommended&nbsp; you not eat more than 0.9 grams of protein&nbsp;&nbsp; for every pound you weigh. This means that&nbsp; a 150-pound person should not consume more&nbsp;&nbsp; than 135 grams of protein in a day. And this&nbsp; is the very upper limit. That being said,&nbsp;&nbsp; if you are someone who is extremely active&nbsp; or an athlete, you can probably get away&nbsp;&nbsp; with eating slightly more protein as your&nbsp; body will use it to repair and build muscle.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is when the body becomes oversaturated&nbsp; with protein that problems start to arise. There are some key steps you should take&nbsp; before starting a high-protein diet,&nbsp;&nbsp; especially if you are going to try and&nbsp; consume the upper limits for your body weight.&nbsp;&nbsp; The first is to discuss the decision&nbsp; with your doctor. They will likely want&nbsp;&nbsp; to run some tests just to make sure your&nbsp; kidneys are healthy and in working order. You will also need to do your research.&nbsp; We have tried to provide you with some&nbsp;&nbsp; of the best food options to eat while on a high&nbsp; protein diet, but there are many other choices.&nbsp;&nbsp; Depending on where you live and what type of&nbsp; foods you like, there are likely other sources of&nbsp;&nbsp; protein that might be better for you. You can also&nbsp; talk to a nutritionist who may be able to give you&nbsp;&nbsp; further guidance. It is important to remember&nbsp; that although some meats have higher amounts of&nbsp;&nbsp; protein than other foods, fish, nuts, and beans&nbsp; can all be healthy supplements to any meal. Regardless of what high-protein foods&nbsp; you decide to eat, you should still&nbsp;&nbsp; include fruits and vegetables in your diet.&nbsp; They may not have as much protein as meat,&nbsp;&nbsp; but they are rich in vitamins&nbsp; and minerals your body needs. You also should identify exactly why you are&nbsp; starting your high protein diet. If your goal&nbsp;&nbsp; is to lose weight, you don’t need to consume as&nbsp; much protein as someone who is working to bulk&nbsp;&nbsp; up and gain muscle. Or maybe you are just planning&nbsp; for the future and want to have a healthier body&nbsp;&nbsp; in the long run. There are many benefits to eating&nbsp; a high protein diet, but there can also be risks.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is why it never hurts to discuss your&nbsp; dietary plan with medical professionals. Now watch “What Happens To Your Body&nbsp; When You Don't Eat (Fast).” Or check&nbsp;&nbsp; out “What Happens To Your Body If You Never Move.”

- 24 High Protein Foods That You Should Eat Regularly: Kind: captions Language: en Hey there viewers! Proteins are the building blocks of your body that provide energy when needed, help bulk up on the muscle, lose fat and eventually help us stay healthy. They’re a win win! But what makes them unique when compared to the other macronutrients is that the body can’t store them the same way it stores fat and carbohydrates. Meaning it’s essential to eat enough protein every day. The amount depends on the age and weight of an individual. But apart from eating enough of it, it is equally important to eat a variety of it as well. This is because each kind of protein has its own amino acid profile. There are a wide range of foods out there which can help you reach your protein goals. And in today’s video, we will tell you what they are. From tempeh, steak, quinoa, banana to black beans and more, watch till the end to learn about all of them. Eggs: Eggs are an excellent source of protein,&nbsp; particularly egg whites.&nbsp; They are low in cholesterol and high in protein. They are also low in fat, which makes them the perfect food for a healthy and nutritious breakfast. One cup of egg whites contains 26 grams of protein. This will also provide you with energy and essential amino acids. What’s your favourite way to eat eggs? Is it boiled? Scrambled? Or anything else? Tell us quickly down below in the comments section! Tofu: ¼ block of tofu contains 12.8 grams of protein and 117 calories. It is a great alternative to meat and is used in a variety of dishes. Apart from proteins, tofu is also rich in magnesium, iron, and other essential nutrients. Like other soy foods, it reduces heart diseases by lowering bad cholesterol and promoting good cholesterol. Pork Chops: 1 pork chop contains 39 grams of protein. It is an excellent source of zinc as well. This mineral is essential for performing&nbsp; numerous reactions in your&nbsp; body. And the deficiency of zinc can lead to night blindness and a weakened immune system. Canadian bacon, another form of pork, contains 15 grams of protein for every 3 ounces serving. And chorizo, a spicy Spanish pork sausage, has 21 grams of protein for every 3 ounce serving. Tempeh: Made from soybeans that are fermented and pressed into a block, tempeh is high in protein, prebiotics and other nutrients. Because it’s more compact than tofu, it’s higher in protein. A three ounce serving will give you 15 to 16 grams. Tempeh’s firm but chewy texture makes it a superb addition to sandwiches and salads. Or, crumble it to substitute for ground meat in recipes. Greek Yoghurt: Greek yogurt has become such a popular choice because it has twice as much protein as other types of yogurt. 8 ounces of it contains about 23 grams of protein. It's also rich in bone-building calcium and probiotic bacteria, which is great for gut health. Look for plain&nbsp; varieties to keep calories—and&nbsp; your weight—in check. Cottage Cheese: ½ cup serving of this cheese contains about 14 grams of protein. Make cottage cheese your go-to food for a healthy late-night snack. It's also high in casein, a slow-digesting dairy protein. Slow-digesting protein feeds your muscles all night so they don't catabolize, and it keeps you from waking up starving at 3 a.m. Halibut Fish: Among white fish species, halibut reigns supreme when it comes to the protein you need to build muscle. Each 3-ounce serving also has a mere 2 grams of fat and 23 grams of protein, making it an even better catch. Pacific halibut is generally considered a more sustainable choice than Atlantic. Yellowfin Tuna: Tuna delivers a boatload of easily digested, high-quality protein. You'll also benefit from the healthy amount of vitamin B and the potent antioxidant selenium, making it a great nutrition choice. 3 ounces of this fish is loaded with around 25 grams of protein. When possible, look for troll- or pole-caught tuna, which are considered the most sustainable options. Interested yet? Make sure you join our millions of followers, and hit that “subscribe” button for all our great Bestie content! Steak: Leaner cuts of steak provide a fantastic 1 gram of protein for every 7 calories; rib eye, on the other hand, delivers roughly 1 gram of protein for every 11 calories. So you can get around 23 grams of protein from a 3 ounce steak. Plus, round steak is considered one of the more economical cuts. Leaner cuts like round and loin will become drier than the Sahara with overcooking, so cook them quickly over high heat to medium-rare. Peanut Butter: Though not as trendy as other nut butters like almond, peanut butter still leads the way in the protein department. 2 tablespoons have about 8 grams of protein. Make sure to watch labels for sugar, though. Natural versions made from just peanuts are best. Some stores even let you grind your own. If you're working to get your weight in check, look for peanut butter powder, which has less fat but the same protein content. You can even use the powder for baking. Broccoli: Broccoli is a plant-based protein which has eight out of the nine amino acids essential for the body. You can steam or stir fry it–both of which are excellent ways to enjoy this cruciferous vegetable. 100 grams of it contains about 3 grams of protein. It is also rich in fibre that makes you feel full and reduces appetite. Chia Seeds: 1 ounce of chia seeds contains 4.4 grams of protein. They are rich in fiber and omega-3s and offer a host of benefits. The seeds also offer a superb energy boost. They deliver a massive amount of nutrients with few calories. Chia seeds also improve certain blood markers, which can cut the risk of heart disease and type 2 diabetes. Chicken breast: Chicken breast is an ideal muscle-building food. It is cost-effective, easy to cook and is rich in protein. It is also high in selenium, which protects the cells of the body from free radical damage that is usually seen during a workout. About 100 grams of chicken breast gives 32 grams of protein. Quinoa: You can call quinoa a power seed as it is rich in nine essential amino acids, proteins, fibre, magnesium, calcium, vitamin B and E, potassium, iron and much more. It is not only nutritious but also delicious. You can make quinoa salads or quinoa rice and consume it on a daily basis. About 100 grams of cooked quinoa contains five grams Potatoes: The humble spud isn't known for being a health food due to its many unhealthy incarnations. But it's actually a wholesome addition to your diet. Just one russet potato contains 8 grams of protein, more potassium than a banana and is a good source of fiber. Other varieties like red or sweet potatoes don't contain as much protein, but they still can contribute to your daily intake goal. Almonds: Almonds are a popular type of tree nut. They are rich in essential nutrients, including fiber, vitamin E, manganese, and magnesium. 1 ounce of them contains about 6 grams of proteins. Lentils: Lentils are among the world’s best sources of plant-based protein, and they’re an excellent choice for vegetarians and vegans. One cup or 198 grams of boiled lentils contains 18 grams of protein. They are also high in fiber, magnesium, potassium, iron, folate, copper, manganese, and various other nutrients. Pumpkin Seeds: 1 cup of the seeds contains 12 grams of protein. The seeds are also rich in zinc that enhances immunity. This very zinc even improves prostate health and prevents prostate cancer in men. Pumpkin seeds can&nbsp; also improve insulin&nbsp; regulation, thereby preventing diabetes. Apart from supplying your body with proteins, pumpkin seeds also contain a variety of nutrients which can do your body a world of good. Learn more about its benefits by watching this video titled “What Happens To Your Body If You Eat Pumpkin Seeds Daily” Now, back to High Protein Foods That You Should Eat Regularly Cauliflower: 1 large cauliflower head contains 17 grams of protein. Another important nutrient this vegetable is rich in is choline. This nutrient enhances memory and learning, improves sleep, and helps with muscle movement. Choline also aids in the transfer of nerve impulses and assists in fat absorption. Banana: 1 large banana contains 1.5 grams of protein. They are also very good sources of potassium. They relax the walls of the blood vessels and lower blood pressure. The fruit can also be a good food to be taken during diarrhea – as electrolytes like potassium are lost during diarrhea, and banana helps replenish the mineral. Edamame: These lightly boiled or steamed soybeans — often served still in their shell — make a great snack or appetizer. One cup of edamame packs 18 grams of protein. The even better news? Whole soy is a complete protein, which means it provides all the amino acids your body needs but can’t make on its own. Oatmeal: Oatmeal is a whole grain and coarse flour it used to eat in breakfast. 100 grams of oatmeal can provide you 17 grams of protein. About 300 grams of it can fulfill the daily requirement of protein. Black Beans: While white beans are packed with protein at around 16 g per cup, they don't even stand a chance against black beans. Just a single cup of these beauties contain nearly 40 grams of protein. That's more than double the amount of any white bean you'll come across. Spinach: With 1 gram of protein per cup of spinach, it's no wonder Popeye had such big muscles. Aside from the protein, spinach is also high in vitamins A and C, folate, and magnesium. It also contains carotenoids that protect the body against heart disease and certain cancers. While proteins are very important for your body, there are other nutrients like vitamins and minerals which you should be eating regularly as well. Learn more about what these foods are by watching: Mineral Rich Foods That You Should Eat Regularly Or Vitamin C Rich Foods That Are Natural Fat Burners So go ahead and watch one or both of these videos for maximum benefits. What do you prefer to fulfil your protein requirements and why, food or powders? Let us know in the comments section below!

When you eat protein, your&nbsp; body’s metabolism shoots up automatically,&nbsp;&nbsp; helping you burn fat. Protein is by far the&nbsp; most filling macronutrient. Eat adequate&nbsp;&nbsp; protein and you will have glowing skin,&nbsp; strong hair and a lower risk of arthritis.&nbsp; Now, when we talk of protein foods, the first&nbsp; thing that comes to our mind is egg. With 6 grams&nbsp;&nbsp; of protein in just 1 egg, it seems promising.&nbsp; However, commercial eggs aren’t as healthy as&nbsp;&nbsp; they should be. You must be wondering why? First,&nbsp; Modern studies have shown that the eggs that are&nbsp;&nbsp; produced by hens living in adverse conditions&nbsp; are significantly low in nutrition. Second,&nbsp;&nbsp; hens are constantly injected with antibiotics&nbsp; to prevent them from getting sick. Eating such&nbsp;&nbsp; eggs have made a large population&nbsp; in India resistant to antibiotics.&nbsp; So let’s talk about 5 Vegetarian protein&nbsp; foods that are better than egg. At&nbsp; #5 is Urad dal Just 1 katori of urad dal will give&nbsp;&nbsp; you 15 grams of protein. And its not just high in&nbsp; protein but is high in 3 vital micronutrients as&nbsp;&nbsp; well. Magnesium, Manganese and Zinc. Magnesium&nbsp; relaxes the muscles. Manganese provides body&nbsp;&nbsp; strength. Zinc helps boost testosterone. No&nbsp; wonder, in Ayurveda, Black urad dal is called&nbsp;&nbsp; Maash, that which helps build muscle. Urad dal is&nbsp; a bit heavy to digest but if you add hing and tej&nbsp;&nbsp; pattar while making it, it gets absorbed easily.&nbsp; Another option is green moong dal which also has&nbsp;&nbsp; 14 grams of protein but what’s better is that it&nbsp; gets readily digested by a kid or an adult. You&nbsp;&nbsp; can have it as a regular dal, sprouts, cheela&nbsp; or halwa. Black urad dal or green moong dal,&nbsp;&nbsp; eat just 1 katori with 2 chapatis, you will&nbsp; get protein more than 3 eggs. While studies&nbsp;&nbsp; show that eating more than 1 egg a day increases&nbsp; the risk of diabetes by 23%. On the contrary,&nbsp;&nbsp; studies suggest that the eating lentils daily&nbsp; reduce the risk of diabetes by 20%. And be it&nbsp;&nbsp; Matki, Kulthi, Rajma or chholle, Indian kitchens&nbsp; have always understood the importance of this&nbsp;&nbsp; fibre rich protein food. At #4 is Bhuna Chana&nbsp; Just 100 grams of bhuna chana has a whopping&nbsp; 22 grams of protein. And because it is roasted,&nbsp;&nbsp; its free from anti nutrients that hinder with&nbsp; the absorption of protein. Even modern studies&nbsp;&nbsp; have recognised that the quality of its protein&nbsp; is superior to other plant proteins. No wonder,&nbsp;&nbsp; Indian wrestlers swear by this superfood. Rather&nbsp; than lays, you can munch on these. Want to spice&nbsp;&nbsp; it up, make a chat out of it. Just make sure of&nbsp; consuming bhuna chana with the skin so that you&nbsp;&nbsp; get maximum fibre. Powdered bhuna chana is called&nbsp; sattu, a famous drink in India known for its easy&nbsp;&nbsp; absorbability of protein. 1 scoop of sattu&nbsp; has 7 grams of protein. Too less? While whey&nbsp;&nbsp; proteins are loaded with artificial sweeteners and&nbsp; flavours, this comes with loads of Iron, calcium,&nbsp;&nbsp; magnesium and folate. Chana sattu can be used as&nbsp; a filling of pranathas, you can roll cheelas from&nbsp;&nbsp; it or simply make its refreshing drink. Low in&nbsp; glycemic index, 100% natural, this is the real&nbsp;&nbsp; protein powder that you should opt for. At #3 is Milk&nbsp; Just one glass of milk will give you 10 grams&nbsp; of quality protein. Plus point is that milk is&nbsp;&nbsp; rich in vitamin D, B12, CLA and omega 3 fatty&nbsp; acids which is also why it is considered a&nbsp;&nbsp; vegetarian alternative to non vegetarian food.&nbsp; A glass of milk with a scoop of chana sattu,&nbsp;&nbsp; sweetened with a banana can be your desi post&nbsp; workout shake. Several studies have demonstrated&nbsp;&nbsp; that drinking milk after a workout can decrease&nbsp; muscle damage, promote muscle repair, increase&nbsp;&nbsp; strength and even decrease muscle soreness. Make dahi&nbsp;&nbsp; and you get 7 grams of protein in one katori.&nbsp; Overall, ethically sourced milk and milk products&nbsp;&nbsp; are not just rich in protein but are also rich in&nbsp; protein enablers that help you build lean muscle&nbsp;&nbsp; mass and reduce body fat. At #2 is Green Peas&nbsp; Green peas is one of those rare vegetables&nbsp; which is not only high in protein but is also&nbsp;&nbsp; a complete source of protein. You might be taken&nbsp;&nbsp; aback but scientific studies show that&nbsp; it is at par with whey protein. In 2015,&nbsp;&nbsp; 161 males were enrolled in a study. They were&nbsp; divided into 3 groups, pea protein, whey protein&nbsp;&nbsp; and placebo. The study concluded that the muscle&nbsp; growth in pea protein group was equal to that in&nbsp;&nbsp; whey protein group. 1 cup of green peas will give&nbsp; you 9 grams of quality protein plus an opportunity&nbsp;&nbsp; to reduce your blood sugar level. Combine it&nbsp; with Aloo, gobhi, gajar, or pulao, green peas&nbsp;&nbsp; is flexible enough. Imagine matar paneer. Just 1&nbsp; serving will easily give you 25 grams of protein.&nbsp;&nbsp; According to Ayurveda, Green peas is ideal&nbsp; for lunch due to its gassy nature. Although,&nbsp;&nbsp; it can be consumed in dinner as well. - Eat These Foods Daily To Lose Weight Effortlessly: Kind: captions Language: en If you're trying to lose weight but feel stuck, you might just be eating the wrong foods. Here are 10 foods that actually help you burn fat, crush cravings, and support your weight loss goals. And nope, nothing boring or expensive. These are easy everyday foods you'll wish you started eating sooner. Let's go. Number one, eggs. Don't skip this. Eggs are literally one of the best weight loss foods. High in protein, low in calories, and they keep you full for hours. Start your day with two boiled or scrambled eggs and notice how much less you snack. Number two, Greek yogurt. The plain one, not flavored. Packed with protein, low in sugar. It helps control hunger hormones and improves digestion thanks to probiotics. Top it with berries or a tiny bit of honey if you need sweetness, but ditch the flavored versions full of sugar. Number three, oatmeal. It keeps you full and balances blood sugar. But here's the trick. Skip the instant packets. Use plain rolled oats. Cook them with water or milk and add cinnamon or fruits. Great for breakfast and way better than sugary cereals. Number four, leafy greens. Spinach, kale, lettuce. Low in calories, high in fiber and nutrients. Fill half your plate with greens and you'll naturally eat fewer high calorie foods without even noticing. Bonus, they help reduce bloating and improve digestion. Number five, chicken breast or lean protein. This is your fat burning engine. Why? Because protein builds lean muscle and keeps your metabolism active. Grilled chicken, turkey, or even tofu. Just keep it lean, not fried. Number six, apples. They're sweet, satisfying, and full of fiber. An apple before meals can actually help you eat fewer calories overall. Plus, it's a great swap for candy or snacks when cravings hit. Number seven, chia seeds. Tiny but powerful. They expand in your stomach, making you feel fuller for longer. Add them to smoothies, oats, or even water with lemon. They also keep you regular, which matters when you're trying to lose belly fat. Number eight, cucumbers and carrots. These are your go-to snacks. Crunchy, lowal, hydrating, and they keep your mouth busy. Next time you're craving chips, try slicing cucumbers with a pinch of chili powder and lemon. Game changer. Number nine, berries, strawberries, blueberries, raspberries. High in antioxidants, low in sugar. They satisfy your sweet tooth without spiking your blood sugar. Add them to yogurt, oats, or eat a handful as a dessert replacement. Number 10, water with lemon or just cold. This isn't just hydration talk. Water literally helps your body burn more calories and reduces false hunger signals. Start each meal with a full glass. It curbs overeating and helps digestion. Here's the truth. You don't need fancy detoxes or starvation diets. You need the right foods that fuel your body, keep you full, and support your goals. Start out adding just three to four of these foods daily and watch how your cravings drop, your energy goes up, and the weight finally starts to move. If you liked this video, hit that like button, share this with someone who needs it, and subscribe for more videos like this.

definitely should be bioavailable complete protein so if you're doing the ketogenic diet with plant-based protein or just not enough bioavailable protein that's going to tell the brain to keep eating so out of all the macronutrients carbs protein fat protein is the most valuable to the body because we don't store it and there's so many different functions that are dependent on this protein so this really relates to when you start doing more intermittent fasting and prolonged fasting if you're deficient in amino acids going into a prolonged fast you're just not going to feel good and so for some people that don't feel good it wouldn't hurt to take a blend of complete amino acids just to fortify the body now to get more information on the healthy version of the ketogenic diet I put that video
